insight: P1 DDEing (double-drone, engineer opener) is correct in many sets, but P2 almost never has DDE as their correct opener. When P1 should DDE but P2 should not, P2 should typically go natural Conduit instead.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of first-player vs second-player asymmetry in opening theory.

insight: P2 has a slight quantitative advantage at the start of the game (extra resource from going second) but suffers from worse build orders, especially for high-economy lines. This means P2 requires more knowledge of alternate drone lines to fully leverage their position.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of P1 vs P2 win rates and why masn6811 performs better as P2.

insight: With Trinity Drone in the set, P2's correct opener is DD/DDC (double drone, double drone conduit), optionally buying a third Engineer if going high economy. P2 should never do DD/DDE with Trinity Drone and no other alternate drones, as Trinity helps P2 but provides no benefit to P1.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of alternate drone opening lines and their P1/P2 asymmetry.

insight: Trinity Drone advantages P2 but not P1, essentially exposing the inherent quantitative advantage P2 already has rather than creating a new one. P1 with Trinity Drone should just play the normal line and accept the disadvantage.
author: masn6811
context: Discussion of Trinity Drone's asymmetric effect on P1 and P2.

insight: Thorium Dynamo locks both players into an obvious fixed opening for the first 4 turns (DD/D1/DD for P1 equivalent), making set-reading largely irrelevant for that phase. A 3-Engineer opener in a Thorium set is approximately equivalent to a 5-Engineer opener in a normal set and is therefore rarely correct.
author: masn6811
context: Expert discussion of Thorium Dynamo's impact on opening theory and game decision space.

insight: When Thorium Dynamo, Chrono Filter, and Antima Comet are all in the same set, P2 is heavily disadvantaged: P2 cannot reach Chrono Filter on their natural Thorium line, is forced to buy natural Conduit instead, and thus falls behind P1 who gets Thorium's economic advantage. This makes it a 'buy Thorium or probably lose' set.
author: masn6811
context: Masn6811 walked through the specific build order trap for P2 when all three units coexist.

insight: Thorium Dynamo is considered by masn6811 to have an unacceptably high power level: it functions like a dominant opener that effectively makes the decision of whether to buy it automatic ('buy it or probably lose'), removes early-game decision variety, and also provides tech-fixing and blue-print support.
author: masn6811
context: Extended expert discussion comparing Thorium Dynamo's dominance to a hypothetical 1BB Deadeye Operative.

insight: One way to understand Thorium Dynamo economically: buying it is equivalent to paying 5E to get 2 Drones this turn while committing to pay 5 for a Conduit next turn — effectively gaining 1 gold one turn earlier and generating an energy from nothing, in addition to its blue-print and flexibility benefits.
author: masn6811
context: Masn6811 explaining why Thorium's economic value is so strong.

insight: In a symmetric Deadeye Operative set (both players going Deadeye), the standard line goes DD/DDE/DDD/BBD/1DDD for P1 and DD/DDE/BBD/1DD/1DDD for P2, followed by continued Drone and Deadeye purchases until gold surplus allows transitioning to other units.
author: p0lari
context: Flakmaniak asked how Deadeye sets typically play out; p0lari provided the standard symmetric line.

insight: Buying Deadeye Operatives once you are past absorb barrier is very poor value, because Deadeye's key advantage is bypassing absorb — paying 5BB for what amounts to a 1-attack unit is inefficient. The exception is if you are overteched with double blue and have no better use.
author: p0lari
context: Discussion of when Deadeye Operative stops being worth buying.

insight: In asymmetric Deadeye games (one player buys Deadeyes, the other attacks aggressively), the Deadeye player wants the game to go long while the aggro player wants a short game. Buying additional Drones when you are the aggro player tends to lengthen the game, which favors the Deadeye player.
author: amalloy
context: Amalloy responding to flakmaniak's question about whether to keep buying Drones as the aggro player against a Deadeye build.

insight: With Vivid Drone in the set, an aggressive opening option for either player is Vivid Drone + 2 Engineers on turn 1. This is unsafe for P1 because P2 can punish with a turn-1 Animus, but is considerably safer for P2.
author: masn6811
context: Masn6811 discussing alternate drone opening lines for P2, using Vivid Drone as an example of a high-econ gamble.

insight: Units with both Prompt and Lifespan behave differently from non-Prompt lifespan units: a Prompt unit with Lifespan 3 placed on the board has its lifespan counter start immediately (the same turn it is bought), while a non-Prompt unit with Lifespan 4 begins its countdown the following turn. This means Prompt lifespan units effectively die one turn earlier relative to when they were purchased than players might expect.
author: p0lari
context: Community discussion about counterintuitive interactions between Prompt and Lifespan keywords.

insight: At high level play, the known P1 vs P2 win rate split is approximately 47% P1 / 53% P2. Among sets with significant alt-drone content, P2's advantage can be considerably larger, and the best players tend to show an even wider P2 performance spread than average.
author: masn6811
context: Masn6811 discussing his unusually high P2 win rate relative to known community data on the P1/P2 imbalance.

insight: Centurion creates more balanced P1/P2 sets by giving both players a semi-efficient line, but this also reduces room for strategic innovation since both players are largely forced into the standard CBBA build sequence. Better players may see their overall win rate compressed in Centurion-heavy metas because skill expression is narrowed.
author: masn6811
context: Masn6811 and awaclus debating whether Centurion makes the game more or less fair.

insight: Plasmafier is considered significantly stronger than Cynestra in practice because it ends games quickly, negating Cynestra's theoretical long-term efficiency advantage. The community meme 'Cynestra: But I'm more efficient in the long term! Plasmafier: What long term?' captures this dynamic.
author: masn6811
context: Masn6811 commenting on an attacker tier list that placed Plasmafier and Vai Mauronax in the same tier.

insight: Master Bot appears to not correctly account for Vai Mauronax's ability to pay attack to freeze a unit, leading it to overbuild defense by 1 more than necessary (blocking 4 above opponent's attack when only 3 above was required).
author: flakmaniak
context: Flakmaniak observed MB consistently over-blocking in a set that included Vai Mauronax, noting it wasn't a granularity issue since Husk was present and MB was buying Rhinos.

insight: Master Bot does not resign at appropriately losing positions and may continue playing until the opponent has literal lethal on the board. A previous version of Master Bot resigned at more appropriate times but was changed after player complaints.
author: masn6811
context: Amalloy noted MB didn't resign on turn 13 when resignation criteria were obviously met; masn6811 confirmed this is a known and longstanding issue.

insight: Master Bot will pursue highly specialized multi-building strategies of questionable value, such as buying triple Blastforge purely to spam Pixies, suggesting it does not adequately evaluate opportunity costs of tech overinvestment.
author: velizar_
context: Velizar_ reported observing Master Bot go triple Blastforge just to spam Pixies in a single observed game.

insight: Master Bot is described as 'pretty competent by bot standards' and noticeably harder to beat than the other available bots. New players are advised to start against bots and can advance considerably in skill before consistently beating Master Bot.
author: consensus
context: Community members advising a new player (cat8472) on how to get started, comparing bot difficulty levels.

insight: Approximately 90% of Prismata games at sub-2000 rating are decided in the first 4 turns; above 2000 rating this is less dominant but still a key insight. This makes correct early-game build-order knowledge disproportionately important relative to mid- or late-game play.
author: masn6811
context: Masn6811 arguing that Thorium Dynamo's dominance in the opening phase is especially problematic given how decisive the first 4 turns are.
